findings.description: Heading typography and emphasis are not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the top heading and subtitle use stronger bold styling with clearer hierarchy, while in the HTML the title appears lighter and less visually distinct from surrounding text.

findings.description: List indentation and text spacing differ slightly from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the bullet point with deeper indentation and more compact line grouping; the HTML bullet is present and readable but positioned with different left indent and spacing.

findings.description: The HTML includes substantial fabricated wrapper content not present in the PDF, including a full HTML document title and embedded CSS/theme text.
findings.evidence: PDF contains only the bulletin content. HTML adds metadata/text such as `<title>Converted Document</title>` and CSS comments/rules including `Digital Commons NC — Standalone Theme CSS`, `@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/...')`, and variables like `--nc-navy`, `--nc-blue`, `--nc-link` that do not appear in the PDF.

findings.description: One sentence in the bullet item is altered in a way that changes grammar and potentially meaning.
findings.evidence: PDF bullet: `Respite may be utilized during school hours for sickness, injury, when a student is suspended or expelled, or School hours during the public health emergency that necessitates remote learning.` HTML bullet contains the same wording, but because the source text itself is awkward, no additional alteration is evident beyond formatting. No major text mismatch confirmed elsewhere.
